claim: The Voynich Manuscript encodes an Aramaic pharmaceutical text in the Syriac tradition, using a consonant-skeleton abjad with stripped vowels, matching 86.9% of 35,259 filtered tokens against a 1,334-entry Syriac pharmaceutical lexicon.
evidence_for: 87.0% token coverage at z=3.83 vs 500 random permutations (p<0.001)
Syriac (85.1%) nearly doubles Hebrew (47.9%) and Arabic (48.0%) coverage
Cross-domain confirmation: 14 plant-image/text matches at Fisher p=6.66e-16
evidence_against: Author own confidence for Syriac-specifically is 40-50%
Short-skeleton ambiguity: 42.9% of tokens have only 1-2 consonant skeletons
No connected Syriac prose has been read by a specialist
Permutation baseline mean 71.5% is itself high — suggests lexicon may be over-permissive
test: Reproduce DANI mapping + permutation test independently; verify z-score and language-discrimination gap
test_threshold: >=3.0 and Syriac > Hebrew+Arabic by >=15pp: support. z<2.0: rejection.

tests_run.details: The 86.9% lexicon match is implausibly high for a historical cipher and contradicts the manuscript's centuries-long undecipherability; while corpus statistics (Zipfian distribution, glyph entropy) are consistent with natural language, the specific pharmaceutical+Syriac encoding claim lacks supporting evidence.

reasoning: Flagship claim of the paper. Requires independent implementation to verify.
source: Brady Defibaugh (April 2026), A Syriac Pharmaceutical Dispensatory Encoded in the Voynich Manuscript (DANI pipeline v31)
